data.title: Boeing CEO says Iran conflict hasn’t disrupted jet deliveries, sees defense upside
data.description:
Boeing Co. (BA) management said ongoing conflict involving Iran hasn't affected its commercial aircraft deliveries, while the company expects heightened geopolitical tensions to support growth in its defense operations.

Chief Executive Kelly Ortberg, speaking Friday at Boeing’s (BA) annual shareholder meeting, said airline customers are still accepting new aircraft on schedule despite concerns that regional instability could ripple through global aviation markets.

Ortberg also struck an upbeat tone on Boeing’s (BA) narrowbody manufacturing plans, saying the company remains on track to raise monthly production of the 737 Max to 47 aircraft this year from the current rate of 42.

The comments suggest Boeing continues to regain momentum in its commercial business as it works to stabilize factory output and improve delivery performance after years of operational setbacks.

At the same time, Ortberg said Boeing’s (BA) defense unit could benefit from the conflict, pointing to potential increases in maintenance work, service contracts and demand for munitions.

The remarks come as aerospace and defense companies monitor whether rising military tensions translate into larger government spending commitments and faster procurement cycles. For Boeing, that could provide an additional tailwind as it seeks stronger performance across both its commercial and defense businesses.

data.title: Most undervalued U.S. oil and gas stocks as crude prices retreat on peace hopes
data.description:
Crude oil prices plunged sharply on Friday as Iran's Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi declared the Strait of Hormuz "completely open" for all commercial vessels for the remaining duration of the ceasefire, the latest sign that a diplomatic resolution to the U.S.-Iran conflict may be within reach. 

The United States Oil Fund (USO) fell over 10% on Friday morning, reflecting the broader sell-off in crude futures. This continues a broader downtrend that began with the initial two-week ceasefire.
 
Oil prices, while still significantly elevated versus pre-conflict levels, are steadily giving back the war premium built up over the past six weeks.

Against this backdrop of falling crude prices, the following list identifies the 13 most undervalued U.S.-listed oil and gas stocks across exploration and production, storage and transportation, refining and marketing, and equipment and services, ranked by Seeking Alpha's valuation grades. The valuation grade reflects how a stock is priced relative to its fundamentals, with A+ indicating the most undervalued and F the most overvalued.

The list is topped by Mach Natural Resources LP (MNR), Northern Oil and Gas, Inc. (NOG), and Plains GP Holdings, L.P. (PAGP), all of which carry an A+ valuation grade. Black Stone Minerals, L.P. (BSM) and PBF Energy Inc. (PBF) follow with A grades.

The list includes a diverse range of companies, from equipment services providers like USA Compression Partners, LP (USAC) to refining firms like PBF Energy Inc. (PBF), all currently ranked highly for their value relative to fundamentals. Midstream operators such as Western Midstream Partners, LP (WES) and Hess Midstream LP (HESM) also appear alongside exploration-focused companies like SM Energy Company (SM) and Talos Energy Inc. (TALO).

Here is the list:

  1. Mach Natural Resources LP (MNR), Valuation grade: A+

  2. Northern Oil and Gas, Inc. (NOG), Valuation grade: A+

  3. Plains GP Holdings, L.P. (PAGP), Valuation grade: A+

  4. Black Stone Minerals, L.P. (BSM), Valuation grade: A

  5. PBF Energy Inc. (PBF), Valuation grade: A

  6. Crescent Energy Company (CRGY), Valuation grade: A-

  7. Delek Logistics Partners, LP (DKL), Valuation grade: A-

  8. Hess Midstream LP (HESM), Valuation grade: A-

  9. Plains All American Pipeline, L.P. (PAA), Valuation grade: A-

  10. SM Energy Company (SM), Valuation grade: A-

  11. Talos Energy Inc. (TALO), Valuation grade: A-

  12. USA Compression Partners, LP (USAC), Valuation grade: A-

  13. Western Midstream Partners, LP (WES), Valuation grade: A-

Energy ETFs: (XLE), (AMLP), (VDE), (XOP), (OIH), and (IXC)

data.title: Biggest stock movers Friday: Travel stocks, NFLX, AFRM, and more
data.description:
Stock futures edged higher Friday while oil slipped sharply to record lows after Iran declared the Strait of Hormuz open for commercial vessels, though President Trump stated the U.S. naval blockade would remain in effect.

Here are some of Friday's biggest stock movers:

Biggest stock gainers

  • Blaize (BZAI) +22% - Shares surged after announcing a contract worth up to $50M with NeoTensr to co-develop AI edge data center infrastructure in the Asia-Pacific region, with the potential to generate significant revenue within the first year. The deal includes co-branded AI servers and full-stack solutions capable of handling high-volume data workloads, underscoring strong demand for edge AI compute.
  • Alaska Air Group (ALK) +14% and Carnival (CCL) +13% - Travel stocks across the board surged on Friday as Brent crude plummeted 12%, slipping below $84 per barrel. The sharp decline in energy prices, triggered by the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz, immediately improved the margin outlook for fuel-sensitive sectors. Major carriers including American Airlines (AAL) +8, United Airlines (UAL) +10, Southwest Airlines (LUV) +9%, Delta Air Lines (DAL) +7%, and JetBlue Airways (JBLU) +7% advanced alongside cruise operators Norwegian Cruise Line Holdings (NCLH) +8%, Royal Caribbean Group (RCL) +10, Viking Holdings (VIK) +6%, and Lindblad Expeditions (LIND) +8%. The positive sentiment extended to the broader hospitality and booking space, with gains in Hyatt Hotels (H) +6%, Marriott International (MAR) +4%, InterContinental Hotels Group (IHG) +5%, Hilton Worldwide (HLT) +3.5%, Airbnb (ABNB) +3%, Booking Holdings (BKNG) +3%, and MakeMyTrip (MMYT) +6%, as investors cheered lower operating costs and improved macroeconomic visibility.
  • Onto Innovation (ONTO) +7% – Shares surged after Stifel Financial upgraded the stock to Buy and raised its price target to $350, citing stronger conviction following qualification of its Gen5 Dragonfly system for advanced packaging. Analysts pointed to likely wins with Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company and SK Hynix, along with multiple growth catalysts tied to next-gen nodes and packaging technologies, supporting an above-industry growth outlook.
  • Affirm Holdings (AFRM) +6% - Shares advanced after Morgan Stanley named it a Top Pick, citing an “overdone” private credit overhang and a strong path for catalysts. The firm sees potential for upward estimate revisions and believes Affirm can sustain 30%+ GMV growth with improving margins, supported by a stable funding backdrop and tightening ABS spreads. Morgan Stanley also flagged the upcoming May Investor Forum as a key catalyst, where updates on growth, margins, and long-term targets are expected, including a potential FY2028 GAAP EPS outlook of $2.50–$3.00, which it views as conservative.
  • Apple (AAPL) +2% – Shares rose after bullish analyst commentary from Bank of America and BNP Paribas, highlighting Apple’s positioning as an “edge AI” leader with on-device intelligence and relative insulation from AI infrastructure volatility. Analysts also pointed to potential iPhone share gains driven by supply chain advantages amid memory constraints, alongside catalysts from its evolving AI ecosystem, including integration with Alphabet Inc.’s Gemini.

Biggest stock losers

  • Badger Meter (BMI) -21% - Shares tumbled, marking their steepest intraday drop since 2008, after the company reported weak Q1 results with an EPS of $0.93, missing estimates by $0.26, and revenue of $202.3M (-9% Y/Y), falling short by $28.6M. The miss was driven by a 10% decline in utility water sales due to project timing delays and softer municipal demand, while flow instrumentation sales slipped 4% amid weaker non-water markets. Adding to investor concerns, the company guided for relatively flat full-year revenue (excluding acquisitions) vs. 2025, signaling limited near-term growth visibility despite some resilience in SaaS, SmartCover, and water quality segments.
  • Netflix (NFLX) -10% - Shares plunged despite a Q1 beat as softer guidance disappointed investors, with the FY2026 revenue outlook maintained at $50.7B–$51.7B (midpoint $51.2B), below consensus, and the operating margin guided at 31.5% vs. 32% expectations, while the Q2 margin is seen declining ~150 bps Y/Y due to higher content amortization. Additionally, co-founder Reed Hastings will not seek re-election as board chairman, adding to the cautious sentiment.
  • Alcoa (AA) -7% - Shares dipped after Q1 results missed expectations, with EPS of $1.40 and revenue of $3.19B both below consensus, while the outlook pointed to mixed trends with higher tariff costs and energy pressures offsetting some operational improvements. The company maintained its 2026 production guidance but flagged near-term EBITDA headwinds, even as it explores asset monetization opportunities, including advanced talks to sell its Messina East smelter site for a data center project.

data.title: Badger Meter shares slide after Q1 revenue and EPS miss
data.description:
Badger Meter (BMI) shares slipped nearly 17% on Friday after the company missed Q1 earnings and revenue estimates.

The company reported Q1 earnings per share of $0.93, missing estimates by $0.26, while revenue of $202.03M missed consensus estimates by $28.58M.

The company, which provides utility water smart metering solutions and software technologies and services to the municipal water utility market, stated that its utility water sales declined 10% year-over-year, reflecting project timing and softer short‑cycle municipal customer ordering.

Additionally, sales of its flow instrumentation products decreased 4%, with modest growth in water-related markets more than offset by lower demand in the de-emphasized array of market applications.

Kenneth C. Bockhorst, Chairman, President, and Chief Executive Officer, said, “Entering 2026, we expected project pacing dynamics to favor the back half of the year. Our first quarter results were consistent with that expectation, reflecting comparisons to the prior‑year period when several since-completed projects were still in active deployment. Additionally, we experienced weaker than anticipated short-cycle order rates in the first quarter of 2026.”

Bockhorst added that the company expects revenue to improve sequentially as awarded projects advance into deployment. Increasing project activity and a more normalized mix will support a stronger revenue run-rate as the firm exits 2026, with full‑year revenue, excluding acquisitions, expected to be relatively flat versus 2025.

Badger Meter shares dipped nearly 28% so far this year, underperforming the benchmark S&P 500, which gained nearly 3% during the same period.

data.title: U.S. markets have likely hit a bottom – analyst
data.description:
The market has likely hit a near-term bottom following a “textbook classic” V-shaped recovery from recent geopolitical turmoil, according to Matt Powers, managing partner at Powers Advisory Group.

The Nasdaq’s (COMP:IND) 12-day winning streak—its longest since 2009—and the S&P 500’s (SP500) return to highs in just 15 trading days demonstrate remarkable resilience.

“It tells you the underlying trend is still pretty strong and how resilient this market really is,” Powers told CNBC.

However, Powers cautioned that the market remains highly sensitive to external factors despite the swift recovery. Oil prices (CL1:COM), (CO1:COM), Federal Reserve timing, and potential geopolitical escalations could quickly shift sentiment, even though much of the macro risk appears to be priced in.

“This is still very much a headline-driven market. I don’t think we’re out of the woods yet,” he said.

The rally’s narrow leadership remains a concern for market health. Powers noted that approximately 40% of the gains off the lows have been driven by just five mega-cap names: NVIDIA (NVDA), Microsoft (MSFT), Apple (AAPL), Alphabet (GOOG), (GOOGL), and Amazon (AMZN).

With the equal-weight S&P 500 (SP500) lagging the cap-weighted index, most stocks are not participating in the upward trend, which Powers described as “not the most healthy setup if you’re talking about a durable move higher in the market.”

For the rally to prove sustainable, Powers emphasized that market participation must broaden beyond mega-cap technology stocks.

“Regardless of cap size, I think just again for this rally to work from here, it has to broaden,” he explained, adding that strong earnings reports will be essential to provide a solid foundation for continued growth.

Despite recent volatility, Powers reported that investor sentiment remains surprisingly calm based on his daily client meetings.

“There’s no panic in the system right now… Investors are staying invested,” he observed. This resilience suggests that market participants are maintaining a long-term perspective rather than rushing to cash positions.

Powers advised investors against reacting impulsively to every news headline, particularly during geopolitical pullbacks.

“The biggest mistake, I think, in the markets is trying to time every headline,” he warned, noting that the market’s best days often come immediately after its worst ones, and missing just a handful of those recovery days can cause significant long-term portfolio damage.

U.S. markets tracking ETFs: (DIA), (DDM), (DOG), (DXD), (SDOW), (SPY), (VOO), (IVV), (RSP), (SSO), (UPRO), (SH), (SDS), (SPXU), (QQQ), (QQQM), (TQQQ), (QID), and (SQQQ).

Mag 7 Focused ETFs: (MAGS), (MAGX), (QQQU), and (QQQD).

data.title: GLP-1 trials show mood improvement rather than the ‘Ozempic personality’ – Dr. Angela Fitch
data.description:
Data from randomized controlled trials shows that most patients’ moods actually improve when treated for obesity with GLP-1 medications, according to Dr. Angela Fitch, Knownwell chief medical officer and president of the Obesity Medicine Association.

“The overwhelming data has shown, in randomized controlled trials at least, that most patients’ mood gets better with treatment of their obesity,” Dr. Fitch said in an interview with CNBC.

Responding to a Washington Post story about so-called “Ozempic personality” and social media anecdotes describing emotional flatness, Dr. Fitch said her clinic has not observed these trends.

At Knownwell, where she oversees treatment of more than 20,000 patients across the country, Dr. Fitch noted they closely monitor mood but have not seen an increase in the issues described in recent media reports.

The evidence regarding severe mental health risks has been equally reassuring. Multiple organizations worldwide have examined data specifically related to mood changes and suicidality and found no increase in these concerns. Dr. Fitch noted that the lack of evidence led regulators to recently remove the suicidality warning from the medication labels.

Despite these findings, Dr. Fitch emphasized that patients should not obtain GLP-1 prescriptions “without the comprehensive wraparound care from a clinician that knows what they’re doing and how to help.”

She stressed that weight management has always had the potential to affect mood in various ways, making proper clinical oversight essential regardless of the treatment approach.

When asked whether mental health concerns might limit the GLP-1 market, Dr. Fitch said the patient population qualifying for these medications remains vast. She clarified that gastrointestinal side effects, not mood changes, remain the primary reason patients discontinue treatment.

For patients experiencing feelings of being “meh,” Dr. Fitch recommended a multifaceted approach beyond medication alone.

She pointed to counseling, behavioral management, and increased physical activity as important tools for addressing mood shifts, noting that treating weight requires comprehensive care that accounts for changes in dopamine-related reward systems.

Pharmaceuticals associated with GLP-1: (LLY), (NVO), (VKTX), (GPCP), (AMGN), (PFE), (HIMS)

GLP-1 investment fund ETFs: (OZEM), (HRTS), (THNR)

data.title: Scotiabank expects ~$56M contribution from KeyCorp's Q1 earnings
data.description:
Scotiabank (BNS) expects the net income contribution from its ownership interest in KeyCorp (KEY) to be ~C$77M (US$56M) in its Q2 2026 results, the Canadian bank said Friday.

The contribution represents KeyCorp's Q1 net income, includes acquisition-related and other accounting impacts, is net of the bank's  associated funding costs, and is reported on a one-month lag. Scotiabank will release its earnings for its fiscal Q2 2026, which ends April 20, 2026, on May 27.

Adjusting for the amortization of acquired intangible assets of ~C$8M, Scotiabank's adjusted net income contribution from Key will be ~C$85M, it added.

The Canadian bank acquired a 14.9% stake in KeyCorp (KEY) in late 2024, giving Key the opportunity to reposition its balance sheet as higher interest rates had reduced the value of some securities it held.

data.title: STMicroelectronics, TXN get upgrades, while NXP Semiconductors cut at Mizuho
data.description:
Mizuho upgraded ratings of STMicroelectronics (STM) and Texas Instruments (TXN), while downgrading NXP Semiconductors (NXPI).

The firm upgraded STMicroelectronics to Outperform from Neutral and raised the price target to $48 from $32. Texas Instruments was upped to Neutral and Underperform, and its price target was increased to $215 from $160. NXP Semiconductors was cut to Underperform from Outperform, and its price target was reduced to $188 from $255. In addition, Mizuho kept Mobileye's Global (MBLY) rating at Neutral but cut the price target to $8 from $11.

STMicroelectronics (STM) and Texas Instruments (TXN)

Analysts led by Vijay Rakesh see STMicroelectronics benefitting from tailwinds in AI server power management and silicon photonics, or SiPho, ramps, while also noting tailwinds from physical AI over the long term with self-driving and humanoid robots and increasing Amazon's LEO satellite deployments. The analysts noted that the increasing demand for LEO satellites is an additional tailwind, helping offset some headwinds in auto/auto in the near term as macro/memory limits vehicle/handset sales in 2026.

"Broad China Price Increases and AI DC [data center] Tailwinds L-T [long term] as Analog Suppliers Look to 800V AI Server Architectures (Positive TXN and STM). We believe strong AI server and Industrial demand is driving TXN and other analog suppliers to raise prices in China 10%+ in April (a big positive driving JunQ upside). We see NVDA GPU and Custom ASIC [application-specific integrated circuit] push to 800V/48V/12V DC AI server architectures driving UPSIDE to power analog demand for TXN/STM," said Rakesh and his team.

In addition, the analysts believe that Texas Instruments and STMicroelectronics are beneficiaries of an Industrials recovery, with: more than 20% revenue in Industrials; exposed to key markets in aerospace and defense, or A&D, medical, and clean energy; and recent segment-specific price hikes of over 10% for stock-keeping units, or SKUs, in power semis for AI server driving upside.

The analysts said that they have seen gradual global manufacturing improvement since the start of 2025 into 2026, as the EU/U.S./China Purchasing Managers Index, or PMI, is now all more than 50 (expansion territory) in 2026 after 2023-2025 saw headwinds.

"We see core industrial demand momentum accelerating into 2H26E, with 1H26E still potentially slightly muted, among reshoring and reindustrializing efforts across N.Am. [North America] and Europe, driving analog from inventory digestion to pockets of improvement," said Rakesh and his team.

NXP Semiconductors (NXPI)

"We believe NXPI saw strong tailwinds for its auto business in 2021-22 following the Covid downturn when it benefited from customers with low inventory and high pricing, and outperformed LVP [Light Vehicle Production]. However, in 2023-25 post-Covid recovery, we saw NXPI auto revenue underperform LVP growth y/y, with customers now sitting on elevated inventories and auto demand fairly muted with growth slowing outside of BEVs. We see headwinds from the memory shortage, as well as ongoing macro problems from raised ASPs and the Iran war, weighing on the auto sector in 2026E, potentially limiting NXPI's revenue growth as autos makes up ~55-60% of its revenue mix," said Rakesh and his team.

The analysts added that NXP has noted tailwinds from AI at the edge, mainly in factory automation and power management, but they believe NXP's AI DC exposure remains limited versus peers, potentially limiting long-term upside from a key analog driver moving forward.

data.title: Badger Meter GAAP EPS of $0.93 misses by $0.26, revenue of $202.3M misses by $28.58M
data.description:
• Badger Meter press release (BMI): Q1 GAAP EPS of $0.93 misses by $0.26.
  • Revenue of $202.3M (-9.0% Y/Y) misses by $28.58M.

  • Utility water sales declined 10% year-over-year, reflecting project timing and softer short‑cycle municipal customer ordering, partially offset by SaaS, SmartCover, water quality and network monitoring performance.

     

  • Sales of flow instrumentation products decreased 4%, with modest growth in water-related markets more than offset by lower demand in the de-emphasized array of market applications.

  • Shares -18%.

data.title: Repay stock jumps on $4.80 apiece acquisition proposal by largest stockholder
data.description:
Repay's (RPAY) largest shareholder, Forager Capital Management, has proposed to acquire the remaining shares of the company in a $4.80 per share all-cash transaction.

Forager currently owns ~13% of Repay's issued and outstanding shares.

Shares were over 30% higher to $4.14 after the market opened on Friday.

The proposed acquisition consideration represents a 75% premium to the company's 30-day volume-weighted average price of $2.75.

"We have access to sufficient financing to complete the transaction," said the shareholder letter by Johnny Wilhelm, partner of Forager.
